Summary: The paper introduces ParetOrch, a framework for Pareto-optimal routing between multi-agent LLM orchestration topologies (flat, hierarchical, debate) based on task characteristics. The approach uses DIT (Decomposability, Iterativeness, Tool Diversity) features to predict per-topology success probability and selects the cheapest topology meeting a configurable quality threshold τ. Evaluation on 84 tasks with 2,016 total runs shows 35.5% cost reduction relative to always-debate while retaining 92.1% of peak quality. Key contributions include the first topology-level Pareto optimization framework, DIT-conditioned routing, comprehensive empirical evaluation, and a crossover insight at DIT magnitude ~0.35.
Strengths: Novel problem formulation bridging cost-aware model routing with topology selection
Extensive empirical evaluation: 2,016 runs across 84 tasks, 3 seeds, 4 baselines
Interpretable DIT features grounded in Simon's near-decomposability theory
Actionable crossover insight (DIT ~0.35) provides practitioners with a deployable rule of thumb
Well-organized paper with clear figures, tables, and derivations
Comprehensive ablation study showing DIT features contribute largest margin (35.5%)
Good statistical rigor with confidence intervals and permutation tests
Weaknesses: Simulated costs from token counting rather than real API billing - significant gap for practical deployment
Simple logistic regression predictor for quality estimation; neural approaches could improve accuracy
Limited scope: only 3 topologies and single base model (GPT-4o)
DIT feature extraction process not adequately detailed; mentions 'one classifier pass' without specifics
Moderate cross-validated routing accuracy (82.1%) - routing failures could degrade quality
Threshold τ = 0.72 selection lacks deeper justification; appears somewhat arbitrary
Synthetic tasks (84 tasks) may not match production task distributions
No analysis of failure modes when routing decisions are incorrect
Missing comparison with more sophisticated routing policies or learned predictors
Questions: Can you provide more details on the DIT feature extractor? What classifier is used, and how is it trained?
Why choose logistic regression over more sophisticated models (e.g., gradient boosting, neural networks) for quality prediction?
How sensitive are the results to the choice of base model? Would the topology rankings change with smaller models?
The paper mentions 82.1% cross-validated accuracy - what are the failure modes? When does the router make incorrect decisions?
What happens when routing decisions are wrong? Is there analysis of quality degradation for misrouted tasks?
How do you determine the optimal τ for different application contexts? Is there guidance for practitioners?
Could the DIT crossover at 0.35 be better justified with theoretical analysis beyond empirical observation?
Real API costs involve latency, rate limits, and batching effects - why were simulated costs used and how would results change?
Limitations: Costs are simulated from token counting, not real API billing, which may not reflect actual production costs
DIT feature extraction requires a classifier pass per task, adding overhead not accounted for in the latency analysis
Limited to 3 topologies and single base model (GPT-4o); generalizability to other settings unclear
Logistic regression predictor is simple; more complex predictors might improve routing accuracy
Synthetic task evaluation suite of 84 tasks may not match production workload distributions
Routing accuracy of 82.1% means ~18% of tasks are misrouted, potentially degrading quality
Fixed quality threshold τ requires manual tuning; no automated adaptation mechanism
